For years, AI infrastructure has been the leading theme.  
And there is no reason to think that has changed.  
It has only accelerated.  
 
Chips: $ARM $KLAC  
 
Foundry: $TSM $INTC  
 
The AI infrastructure story is no longer limited to GPUs—it is expanding across the entire semiconductor value chain. ARM is a key beneficiary of the AI era through its CPU architecture, powering everything from mobile devices to data centers. KLAC is a leader in semiconductor process control and yield management, making it a critical enabler of advanced chip production. 
 
On the manufacturing side, TSM remains the backbone of advanced semiconductor fabrication and the primary producer of many AI chips. INTC represents a potential turnaround story and a major player in domestic manufacturing expansion. 
 
While investors have focused heavily on GPUs, the next phase of AI growth may be driven by the broader ecosystem of chip design, manufacturing equipment, and foundry capacity. The more AI scales, the greater the demand for advanced process technology. 
 
If you could only own one AI infrastructure stock for the next 3–5 years, would you choose ARM, KLAC, TSM, or INTC? Why?

Intel’s 18A-P node hitting risk production on schedule is starting to ripple through the entire semi equipment space. 
 
What I find interesting is not the headline itself, but what it implies underneath. 
 
Every jump in node complexity means one thing: more tools per wafer, not fewer. 
 
$ASML still the only EUV gatekeeper Intel relies on 
$AMAT benefits as deposition + etch steps multiply 
$KLAC gets tailwind from tighter defect control + inspection demand 
 
This is less about Intel “going advanced” and more about the industry structurally consuming more equipment per cycle. 
 
When process nodes shrink, complexity doesn’t disappear  it compounds. 
 
Feels like capex intensity is quietly turning back up across the chain.

$INTC hitting 18A-P risk production on schedule is feeding directly into the semiconductor equipment bid, and the market is quickly repricing what that means for the entire advanced node stack. 
 
As nodes shrink, process complexity doesn’t scale linearly - it compounds. That translates into higher tool intensity across EUV, deposition, etch, and metrology. 
 
Key beneficiaries in focus: 
 
$ASML -sole provider of EUV lithography, still irreplaceable at leading edge nodes 
$AMAT - critical deposition + etch systems as layer counts and materials complexity increase 
$KLAC - inspection + metrology demand rises sharply as defect tolerance collapses at smaller geometries 
 
The takeaway: Intel’s node progression isn’t just a company-specific milestone - it’s a utilization tailwind for the entire semi-capex ecosystem. When advanced node risk production steps forward, equipment demand usually follows with a lag.

$LRCX  ASML isn't obviously cheap on absolute multiples, but it often looks relatively attractive or at least fairly priced for its moat compared to peers like $LRCX, $AMAT, and $KLAC when you adjust for its unmatched position, long-term growth durability, and backlog visibility. ASML's High-NA EUV 0.55 NA is transitioning from R&D/pilot phase to early high-volume manufacturing HVM readiness in 2026, with meaningful but still limited adoption so far. It represents a major leap in resolution for sub-2nm nodes, enabling simpler patterning and higher transistor density, but faces hurdles around cost, ecosystem maturity, and alternatives like multi-patterning on Low-NA 0.33 NA tools. Fitch: The US govt’s recent equity investment in $INTC signals an escalation of US industrial policy, potentially creating wide-ranging unintended inefficiencies for the global semiconductor industry although unlikely to result in broad negative credit implications for chip makers 
 
If legally upheld, the transaction may distort capital investments in the US by foundries, including those by Intel & current foundry leader $TSMC & incentivize duplicative supply chains for fabless chip makers.  
 
TSMC, which is completing its own factory in Arizona and has pledged to add capacity to meet US national security objectives, may slow the pace and scale of US investments if it believes the government stake in Intel structurally disadvantages TSMC’s US foundry business.  
 
TSMC’s factories will likely be focused on at least one generation behind the company’s most advanced process technology node but this still provides the US w/ supply that is more competitive than Intel’s current offering.
